title: Red, yellow, purple: Drivers, followyour color to get to the state fair
summary: Red, yellow, purple: Drivers, follow your color to get to the state fair By MOLLY BROWN Daily News reporter The Alaska State Fair is notorious for monster-size vegetables, big-wheel trucks and terrible traffic. With opening day tomorrow, Chuck Landers, the fair's traffic and parking manager, offered advice to ease traffic strain. Landers advised all drivers to follow posted fair signs along the Glenn Highway. These signs will send drivers to color-coded parking lots designed to make leaving the fair easier, he said. If arriving from the south, park in the red lot. If coming from the north, park in purple or yellow lots. To help weekend fairgoers find the best path, maps with exit options will be distributed, Landers said. "When it gets congested those will provide the easiest way out," he …

title: Fires erupt, threaten homes
summary: Fires erupt, threaten homes Residents evacuated near Palmer By DON HUNTER, NATALIE PHILLIPS and S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News reporters Website: Alaska Fire Service (BLM) Website: National Weather Service Fire Forecasts Website: National Weather Service Fire Forecasts See related pages: http://fire.ak.blm.gov/ http://www.alaska.net/~nwsar/html/firewx/firewx.html http://www.dnr.state.ak.us/forestry/fireinfo.htm Gusting winds whipped nearly a dozen wildfires to life across Southcentral Alaska Tuesday, with one threatening about 50 homes near Palmer and another burning across an estimated 200 acres of dense woods in Anchorage's Eklutna River Valley. On the Kenai Peninsula, crews raced to put out four smaller wildfires - one near Skilak Lake, one in Kenai and two at Homer. Other brush fires were…

title: Alaska prices dropping
summary: Alaska prices dropping Gas tax hike faces bipartisan balk Daily News wire services By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau JUNEAU - State lawmakers on both sides of the aisle said Wednesday they are wary of Gov. Tony Knowles' plan to double the state's gasoline tax to help secure billions of federal transportation dollars. Some legislators said the state should consider tapping local governments for transportation money or using earnings from the Permanent Fund, before signing off on what would be the first increase in Alaska's gasoline tax since 1961. Others said any plan to double the state's current 8-cent gasoline tax should be accompanied by an explanation for perceived high prices at the pump in Alaska. "A gasoline tax effects every Alaskan, every one. It even touches more people…

title: Kodiak man finds bomb near bridge
summary: Kodiak man finds bomb near bridge By SHEILA TOOMEY Daily News reporter Kodiak heavy equipment operator Chris Deater was hunting deer and, incidentally, keeping an eye out for fishing lures suitable for one of his garage sales. Near the White Sands Beach bridge, northwest of town on Monashka Bay Road, he spied a bright orange Pixie through his binoculars. When he went to pick it up, he noticed an odd looking cylindrical device, so odd that he marked it with spray paint and reported it to the Alaska State Troopers, said his wife, Julia. After confirming the presence of what appeared to be a 141/2-inch pipe bomb, the troopers on Saturday evening flew in a unit of experts from the U.S. Army 716th Explosive Ordnance Disposal Company at Fort Richardson. The army experts disarmed the device, whic…

title: First snow marks start of winter, cheechako demolition derby
summary: First snow marks start of winter, cheechako demolition derby By Mike Doogan A lot of snow fell on the day of the first snow that year. I was a young man, living and working downtown. The wind was blowing a little there, as it always does, pushing the snow around. I was walking to work, in a hurry, and put an unwary foot in a drifted-over driveway cut. My feet shot straight up into the air, I hung there for a moment, and came to earth again on my left shoulder. The people who came over to see if I was all right said it was the most spectacular fall they had ever witnessed in person. I thought about that fall last Tuesday, as I do every year on the day enough snow falls to stick. As I looked out my kitchen window at a world suddenly white, I even rubbed my left shoulder a little. I'd like to…

title: Court to reconsider religious rights of landlords
summary: Court to reconsider religious rights of landlords By BOB EGELKO The Associated Press SAN FRANCISCO - The nation's largest federal appeals court agreed Tuesday to reconsider a ruling allowing landlords to refuse to rent to unmarried couples for religious reasons. A panel of the 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals ruled 2-1 in January that enforcement of local and state laws against housing discrimination based on marital status would violate the religious freedom of two Anchorage landlords. But the full court said Tuesday a majority of its 21 active judges had voted to set aside the January ruling and refer the case to an 11-judge panel for a new hearing at a date not yet scheduled. The rehearing had been sought by the state of Alaska and the city of Anchorage, with support from attorneys gen…

title: Miracle of the desert
summary: Miracle of the desert 'Stroke of luck' led trackers to adventurer lost in Australia By SUSAN GILMORE The Seattle Times Alaskans are famed for gritty survival odysseys. But nobody was prepared for the likes of Robert Bogucki, who created an international stir when he walked into the rugged Australian outback last July with a Bible and little else, and disappeared. After weeks of no word and a cold trail, Australian police abandoned hope. A U.S. search crew, joined by veteran Northwest tracker Joel Hardin, was dispatched at the request of Bogucki's agonized family. Their unstated mission was to find a body: Officials presumed Bogucki was dead. Only those who knew him best - his family, girlfriend, close Alaska friends - clung to the belief that this was a man who could wrestle the odds and w…

title: Planners reveal city's dream of future
summary: Planners reveal city's dream of future Residents favor neighborhood diversity By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter Over the past few months, hundreds of people have attended community meetings and told city planners what kind of city they want Anchorage to become. At a town meeting attended by more than 200 people Wednesday night, city planners summarized those comments and unveiled a vision of Anchorage based on what people said they want. The vision is a mixture of two growth scenarios planners had earlier outlined for the public: one that focuses on more improvements downtown, and one that mixes schools, parks, businesses and shopping in neighborhoods throughout Anchorage. Residents said they want the city to encourage development in Ship Creek and like the idea of more apartments a…

title: Robber dares to target hotel full of cops
summary: Robber dares to target hotel full of cops Dozens of officers interrupt training to arrest suspect By LIZ RUSKIN Daily News reporter Of all the hotels in Anchorage, a robber on Thursday chose to stick up the downtown Holiday Inn - the very hotel that is hosting a two-week police training conference. Forty police officers from across Alaska were in three meeting rooms just off the lobby when, according to charging documents, Floyd E. Brown walked into the hotel wearing a ski mask. "He's definitely a candidate for America's dumbest criminal," said Holiday Inn general manager Stephen Horton. Brown, 24, walked up to the front desk about 10:30 p.m., according to the charges. "He said, 'Gimme all your money and you won't get hurt.' He was fumbling in his jacket like he had a gun and kind of givin…

title: Rebel lawmakers face penalties
summary: Rebel lawmakers face penalties By LIZ RUSKIN Daily News reporter House leaders met Monday to decide the punishment for four conservative legislators who defected from the Republican majority caucus in September. The Committee on Committees, without dissent, recommended that the four - Scott Ogan of Palmer, Vic Kohring of Wasilla, Jerry Sanders of Anchorage and John Coghill of Fairbanks - lose their chairmanships and their seats on important committees. Ogan and Sanders would be off the Resources Committee, which they co-chair. Kohring would lose his seat on the powerful Finance Committee. Coghill would keep his seat on the Health, Education and Social Services Committee, but would no longer be co-chairman. To take effect, the Legislature as a whole must act on the recommendation after its …

title: Letters to the editor
summary: Letters to the editor No vote could kill fund A no vote could be the end of the Alaska Permanent Fund dividend as we know it and will ensure that Alaskans pay more in income tax. It is estimated that last year Alaskans paid the IRS about $180 million in taxes on the PFD and now we want to add a state income tax? Back in 1978 when we had an income tax in Alaska, the highest marginal rate paid by individuals was 14.5 percent. Do we want to go back to that? A yes vote will use a portion of the Permanent Fund earnings to help pay for essential services such as education, public safety and road maintenance. The plan will also ensure that we all receive a dividend in the future with the fund staying intact. - Allen Bingham, CPA Anchorage Cut, cut, cut Last week, House Speaker Brian Porter made t…

title: GPS owners advised they may fail soon
summary: GPS owners advised they may fail soon The Associated Press KENAI - A tiny receiver the size of a cigarette package can pinpoint your position, give or take a football field, anywhere on earth. But owners of Global Positioning System receivers need to take care later this week when a programming glitch could cause some to fail. Most GPS receivers will continue to work, said Cmdr. Leonard Allen, chief of operations planning for the Coast Guard Navigation Center in Alexandria, Va. Older versions are particularly likely to fail sometime between Thursday and Sunday. "We're advising people that they should be aware of the problem, just in case they are relying on it for safety or for navigation," he said. "It's more of a problem for older receivers, but that doesn't mean it's not a problem for n…

title: Eco-group teaches the ropes
summary: Eco-group teaches the ropes Activists practice protest strategies By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Mat-Su Bureau HATCHER PASS ROAD - The woman who goes by her initials - SPBK - was 15 feet up a birch tree, her long blond pony tail swingingas she double-checked the nylon strap looped around the tree's trunk. Siri Pritam Bhagvati Khalsa had spent the morning in tree-climbing practice using only nylon ropes and a waist harness. Her instructors hoped she might use her skills to protest for some environmental cause. But Khalsa, a 21-year-old freshman at University of Alaska Anchorage, wasn't sure she had something to protest. "I hear things that sound interesting," she said, as she inched her way up the tree. "But I didn't have a cause when I got here, and I still don't have one." Khalsa and abou…

title: Drowning feared in deaths
summary: Drowning feared in deaths Separate accidents claim Selawik, Anchorage men By JULIA O'MALLEY Daily News reporter A Selawik man and an Anchorage man apparently drowned in separate accidents over the July Fourth weekend. Herbert Jones, 33, of Selawik died in a boating accident near his Northwest Alaska hunting and fishing camp. Jones was last seen Saturday night driving in circles in his 16-foot skiff on a tributary of the Selawik River. Troopers said Jones apparently fell from the boat and drowned. He was not wearing a life jacket. Family members who were celebrating Independence Day with him at his camp told Alaska State Troopers he had consumed about a gallon and half of home-brew during the evening, trooper Eric Olsen said. The other relatives, including Jones' wife, also had been drinkin…

title: Hope dredge runs into roadblocks
summary: Hope dredge runs into roadblocks By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - Two state agencies have asked to review plans to use a giant dredge to sort gold from Resurrection Creek gravel near Hope. The state Division of Governmental Coordination said it should have been asked to review the project a year ago to make sure the mine complies with the Alaska Coastal Zone Management Plan. It has asked the Hope Mining Co. to submit an application and copies of any existing permits. Once those are filed, the agency will begin a 50-day review, said Tom Atkinson, a project review coordinator for the agency. The state Department of Fish and Game's Habitat Division also wants to visit the mine site and review the project. The division is concerned about the amount of dirty water discharged…

title: Fire victims vow to 'press on'
summary: Fire victims vow to 'press on' By SHEILA TOOMEY Daily News reporter Gray skies, black ash and Monday. Time for the victims of three suspicious weekend fires to tote up losses and get back on track. Barry Dannehy ordered new inventory and said how glad he is that no one got injured by the flames that destroyed his South Anchorage furniture warehouse early Saturday morning. "I didn't lose anything I can't replace, no one's life and health," said Dannehy, co-owner of Morgan's Home Furnishings. "Anything else I can deal with." The warehouse, off 64th Street, burned to the ground in a blaze that fire investigators have not officially labeled arson. It was one of three fires reported in Anchorage on Saturday between 1:29 and 2:51 a.m. Dannehy estimated the loss at $1.2 million for the building a…

title: Ball-field vandalism escalates; truck drags man about 300 feet
summary: Ball-field vandalism escalates; truck drags man about 300 feet By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter Rick Pettit knows kids sometimes will go bad. Pettit has coached residents of the McLaughlin Youth Center and the Jesse Lee Home in Anchorage and, through baseball, has shown wayward youth a road back. Most kids are good, Pettit says with quiet faith. But Monday evening, he said, he met an incorrigible teen, one who dragged him along the side of a pickup for about 300 feet. Pettit, 55, said he believes the youth was trying to kill him. Anchorage police said a blond boy of about 16 dragged Pettit through one of the gravel parking lots of the Abbott-O-Rabbit Little League complex on the Hillside after Pettit, a field caretaker, and a second league official had confronted the boy. The teen, with …

title: Bristol Bay crush might dent prices
summary: Bristol Bay crush might dent prices By WESLEY LOY Daily News reporter NAKNEK - Chances look good that commercial fishermen in Bristol Bay will harvest more than 20 million fish this summer, roughly double the number they netted in each of the last two years. Now that the string of disastrous seasons is broken, however, fishermen are worrying about something else - price. Fishermen usually have to wait until after the season, until about the end of July, to get a clear answer from processors as to how much per pound they'll receive for their catches. Going into the season, market analysts were pretty upbeat that Bristol Bay red salmon would fetch more than last year's average of $1.20 a pound. "There's no reason that we should get less than a dollar and half a pound, bare minimum," said Joh…

title: Builder gives up battle on Hillside
summary: Builder gives up battle on Hillside By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter Pressured by a citizens group, an Anchorage developer who won permission to change the restrictions on his Hillside subdivision has returned to his original plan. The developer, John Berggren, negotiated a settlement last week with the Anchorage Citizens' Coalition, which watches over land use decisions. The coalition had appealed a decision by the Platting Board that relaxed the rules governing how lots in the Goldenview Gate subdivision off Goldenview Drive could be cleared and graded. The settlement ended the group's appeal, and as part of the agreement, Berggren reimbursed the group's $258 appeal fee. The coalition called the settlement a victory and said Anchorage developers need to realize that someone is wa…

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Langdon goes to Cavs Alaska-born star grabbed in NBA draft's first round By VAN WILLIAMS Daily News reporter Trajan Langdon, the pure-shooting guard from Anchorage who earned a college degree and national recognition for his jump shot, will now make a living out of torching opponents after being selected Wednesday in the first round of the National Basketball Association draft. Langdon, 23, became the first Alaska-born player to be selected in the NBA draft when he was picked No. 11 by the offense-starved Cleveland Cavaliers, one of the league's lowest scoring teams last season. "It's still unbelievable to me," Langdon told KTUU-TV. "I mean you obviously dream about it, but coming into today I just wanted to be first round. It's amazing." Going as high as No. 11 was a surprise, considering…

title: Dozens save couple before van explodes
summary: Dozens save couple before van explodes Head-on crash near Seward kills driver of other car By LARRY CAMPBELL Daily News reporter A tour bus driver, his passengers and dozens of other people saved a Florida couple from their burning van north of Seward after the couple had collided with another vehicle Saturday. The driver of the other vehicle later died. Princess Tours driver Greg Vancil said he and others formed a human chain to help the husband and wife climb from their demolished vehicle, which lay in a roadside ditch after the head-on collision. Bill Driggers, 77, and wife Eleanor Driggers, of Eustis, Fla., were reported in fair condition Sunday at Providence Alaska Medical Center, in Anchorage. Aaron Mark Jones, 36, of Trapper Creek died Saturday at Providence. The collision occurred …

title: 2 Wasilla-area house fires raise suspicions
summary: 2 Wasilla-area house fires raise suspicions By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Mat-Su Bureau PALMER - State fire officials are investigating whether someone intentionally set blazes Friday that destroyed two Wasilla-area homes, including one being built by a local church. The fires occurred just hours apart and were both located just off Knik-Goose Bay Road. No one was injured. The fires come just days after an arsonist destroyed the Butte Community Center east of Palmer. Officials say they don't know whether all three blazes are linked but they're investigating the possibility. "It's something you've got to wonder about," Mat-Su Public Safety Director Kevin Koechlein said. Both fires Friday occurred in the early morning hours. The first, reported at 1:30 a.m., destroyed a one-story home off R…

title: State alerts landowners to trail plans
summary: State alerts landowners to trail plans The Associated Press FAIRBANKS - The Knowles administration has notified more than 8,000 property owners - most in the Matanuska and Susitna valleys - that the state intends to record public trails crossing their private land. State land managers describe the letters as a low-cost way to raise awareness about access routes the state has claimed under the provisions of an 1866 mining statute. "To me, as a land manager, I feel I have a certain responsibility to let people know where these trails are," Nancy Welch, regional chief for the Department of Natural Resources, said Wednesday. The Knowles administration has opposed the Legislature's insistence that the state record the trail easements on property titles. That has prompted some critics of the adm…

title: Albright to visit Alaska
summary: Albright to visit Alaska Speech, fishing on her itinerary The Associated Press Secretary of State Madeleine Albright plans a quick visit to Alaska this week, but not so quick that she will pass up the chance to wet a line in the Kenai River. Albright will arrive Thursday afternoon at Elmendorf Air Force Base for a stopover while en route to a meeting of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations' foreign ministers in Singapore, state officials said. Gov. Tony Knowles will meet her when she arrives and will accompany her on her half-day whirlwind trip through Southcentral Alaska, the governor's office said. Albright will spend the night in Anchorage and depart Friday for Asia. "Alaskans look to the Pacific Rim for business, culture and education perhaps more than any other state, so it's ce…

title: Villages granted power
summary: Villages granted power Barrow, Chevak to direct child welfare By LISA DEMER Daily News reporter Two Alaska Native villages have won the unprecedented right to take over the sensitive and controversial work of protecting children from abuse and neglect. The Bureau of Indian Affairs has granted the tribal governments in Barrow and Chevak exclusive jurisdiction over Native child custody cases effective Sept. 7. Both villages petitioned the BIA to gain control over children's matters. The decision - made public July 6 - caught both the villages and the state by surprise because they had heard nothing from the BIA since the petitions were filed in 1996 and 1997, state and village representatives said. Barrow and Chevak have little time to prepare for a job that Alaska's social services commissi…

title: Court narrows options
summary: Court narrows options Subsistence suit falls on appeal By DAVID WHITNEY Daily News Washington Bureau WASHINGTON - Any chance of a judicial order to stop the Oct. 1 federal takeover of subsistence fish and game management on federal lands in Alaska ended Tuesday with a federal appeals court ruling. U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia decided that the Alaska Legislative Council - which has the power to act for the Legislature between sessions - lacked standing to sue over the issue. That means that the Legislature's only option for stopping the federal subsistence takeover is to approve a constitutional amendment by Sept. 30 that would restore rural residents' priority claim to fish and game when stocks are short. Tuesday's opinion still could be appealed to the U.S. Supreme C…

title: Senate to revisit plan that would put budget options on ballot
summary: Senate to revisit plan that would put budget options on ballot By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau JUNEAU - The state Senate will begin work today on a long-range fiscal plan by revisiting an earlier proposal to put options before Alaska voters. The approach calls for a special statewide advisory election in September to give residents a yes or no vote on two plans to close Alaska's fiscal gap. Both options on the ballot would use earnings of the Permanent Fund to help balance the state budget. One, proposed by Gov. Tony Knowles in January, also would include a state personal income tax. The Senate Finance Committee is scheduled to take up the bill this morning. But senators emphasized Friday that the measure is only a starting point, and none would predict what the Senate might fi…

title: Wildfire cleanup nears end
summary: Wildfire cleanup nears end Firefighters continued mopping up the remnants of the Eklutna and Palmer wildfires Friday, including using a specially designed heat-detection camera to find remaining hot spots. The fire off the Clark-Wolverine Road in Palmer was contained late Wednesday evening after burning across 150 acres. Light rain Thursday helped in the effort to contain the Eklutna fire, which was deemed 85 percent contained by late Friday and was expected to be fully contained by midnight, according to John See with the state Division of Forestry. That fire burned across an estimated 300 acres. To find the final hot spots, firefighters were relying on a "Thermalcam," which is about the size of a videocamera and is used from a low-flying airplane to detect hot spots that otherwise wouldn…

title: House votes to cap PFD
summary: House votes to cap PFD Proposal includes advisory public vote By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau JUNEAU - The state House, in what many of its members called a historic vote, agreed Saturday to tap Alaska's Permanent Fund to help pay for state government and to place a $1,000 cap on annual dividends for the next three years. In a 27-12 vote, the House also agreed to hold a Sept. 14 statewide advisory election on the long-range fiscal plan and delay implementing the plan until Sept. 30. That means state voters would cast ballots just weeks before the state is to pay out the annual dividends in the first week of October. Without the plan, dividends are expected to be about $1,600 to $1,700. Although the election would be nonbinding, lawmakers agree that if voters reject the plan, th…

title: Alaska general survives Arkansas crash
summary: Alaska general survives Arkansas crash By DAVID HUNN Daily News reporter As American Airlines flight 1402 careened across a runway in Little Rock, Ark., during a Tuesday night thunderstorm, Brigadier Gen. Stephen Korenek was a passenger in row 14. Everything happened too fast to be scared, said Korenek, commander of the Alaska Army National Guard. The MD-80 skidded sideways into a steel light tower and toward the Arkansas River. The impact peeled back the aircraft's skin and broke the plane apart. After it slid to a halt, the plane began to catch fire, and Korenek and other passengers wiggled through emergency exits and gashes in the fuselage. "Either you're dead or you're not," Korenek said. "And if you're not, you figure you've got something to do about it." As of Wednesday night, nine p…

title: Spill recovery produces guidebook to disaster
summary: Spill recovery produces guidebook to disaster By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter While state and oil industry workers struggled to mop up the 11 million gallons of oil spilled by the Exxon Valdez in 1989, no one knew what changes were in store for the communities hit by the disaster or how to prepare for them. In the 10 years since the spill, some of those communities unraveled, individuals struggled with depression, alcohol abuse increased, families fell apart, and businesses struggled to get back on track. Because it was a man-made, or what is known as a technological disaster, there was no handbook on how to help the communities and victims cope and resume their normal lives. To remedy that, the Prince William Sound Regional Citizen's Advisory Council has spent the last several yea…

title: ANCSA returned people to banks of Colville
summary: ANCSA returned people to banks of Colville By BEN SPIESS Daily News reporter Nuiqsut was founded by a group of unlikely pioneers. They were not white settlers plodding west in wagons, but Eskimos driving east on snowmachines. In April 1973, 27 families packed tents, fuel and food atop snowmachines and headed 180 miles from Barrow to the banks of the Colville River. They spent two years living in tents before they built the first houses in town. The village was new, but the Eskimos always considered the Colville River country home. Inupiat Eskimos traditionally ranged across the North Slope. The river had good fishing. The nearby tundra was rich in caribou. Nuiqsut was a seasonal village. Ancient burial sites sit near the village. By the time of the 1968 Prudhoe Bay oil discovery, most Inup…

title: Avalanche kills snowboarding pair
summary: Avalanche kills snowboarding pair Mount McGinnis slide boosts Alaska death toll to 11 this year By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau bureau Colorado Avalanche Information Center, Cyberspace Snow and Avalanche Center, Westwide Avalanche Network See related pages: http://www.caic.state.co.us/ http://www.csac.org/ http://www.avalanche.org/ JUNEAU - Two local snowboarders were killed Tuesday morning when an avalanche with boulder-sized chunks of snow and ice swept them more than 2,000 feet down a prominent peak about 12 miles from downtown. Katrina Winchell, 32, and Matthew Brakel, 33, were found unconscious and partly buried after the slide came to rest around 10:30 a.m. on the west side of Mount McGinnis. They were taken by helicopter to Bartlett Regional Hospital where they were pronounced …

title: Providence to restart talks at last offer
summary: Providence to restart talks at last offer By LISA DEMER Daily News reporter The contract offer rejected last month when Providence Alaska Medical Center nurses went on strike will serve as the starting point for talks scheduled to resume Thursday, hospital officials said. "We can move forward from the last, best offer and see how that goes," said Midge Stafford, Providence chief nurse executive. Registered nurses walked out April 15 because of differences on patient care, working conditions and wages. Ruth Carpenter, a federal mediator based in San Francisco, called the two sides back to the table this Thursday and Friday. Stafford did not say where the hospital was prepared to give but said "there were points that weren't talked about because of how the process had stopped." She said the …

title: Young fears run to add gun laws
summary: Young fears run to add gun laws By DAVID WHITNEY Daily News Washington Bureau Website: Don Young, U.S. Representative for Alaska See related pages: http://www.house.gov/donyoung WASHINGTON - Alaska Rep. Don Young said Monday that the 15 teenagers shot to death at a Colorado high school last month and the six wounded in a school shooting near Atlanta last week are no reason for Congress to retreat on the gun rights of law-abiding citizens. Young said that the deaths have been "overexposed" by the media and that it and the public have targeted the gun industry as the culprit without digging deeper into the social dissolution and collapse of classroom discipline that are behind the shootings. "Tragedies that they were, no one has really addressed the issue of why they occurred," Young said in…

title: Charity to shore up river
summary: Charity to shore up river Fishing fund-raiser aids Soldotna parks By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - Kenai River Sportfishing Inc., which every year holds a celebrity fund-raising fishing tournament, plans to spend another $300,000 to patch up the riverbank and improve parks. Most of it will be spent within Soldotna city parks, where thousands of anglers converge every summer to launch boats or cast flies for salmon. About half the money, $156,000, will be spent in Centennial Park on the southern shore of the river across from downtown Soldotna. It will pay for a trail for anglers as well as an elevated walkway along a damaged portion of the river bank that will be replanted with willows. A stairway for access to the river by shore-based king salmon anglers also will be b…

title: Murkowski plans BP buyout hearings
summary: Murkowski plans BP buyout hearings By BEN SPIESS Daily News reporter Alaska Sen. Frank Murkowski said Friday he plans to hold hearings before the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee on the pending buyout of Atlantic Richfield Co. by BP Amoco. "I share the concerns of Alaskans over jobs and about dependence on a single company," Murkowski said. But the senator said he does not necessarily oppose the $26 billion takeover. "There a lot of opportunities here," he said. "The best way to approach this is to not come in with any preconceived notions." Murkowski said that he may favor tax or royalty incentives to entice investment by smaller, independent companies in North Slope oil fields. He was not specific about how such incentives would work. Consumers and political leaders in Calif…

title: UA's Net speed boosted
summary: UA's Net speed boosted Fiber-optic upgrade planned for October The Associated Press FAIRBANKS - The University of Alaska is getting a new fiber-optic link to the Internet that's 100 times faster than the school's current connection. Technology officials at the university are wowed by the coming upgrade, though they don't yet know what they'll do with it. The fiber-optic link, called OC-12, is scheduled to be working by October, and will give the university one of the fastest Internet connections among the nation's research universities. Steve Smith, the university's chief technology officer, said the speed difference will be staggering. "We were going out to the rest of the country on a dirt road," he said. "Now we're on a superhighway." The news is exciting for university researchers and …

title: Problem bars, development fuel downtown Assembly race
summary: Problem bars, development fuel downtown Assembly race By DON HUNTER Daily News reporter ADN ElectionCentral See related pages: http://www.adn.com/elections/99muni/index.html Assembly District 1 encompasses the oldest neighborhoods in Anchorage, parts of town where streets are still sprinkled with high-ground cottages built in 1915 for railroad bosses, with Tudor-style houses built in South Addition 20 years later, with duplexes and four-plexes erected in Fairview before statehood, with apartment buildings thrown up in Mountain View along the boundary of Elmendorf Air Force Base during the Cold War buildup. Like the neighborhoods, the issues touted by five people who want to be the district's sole representative on the 11-member Anchorage Assembly should be as familiar to voters as a favori…

title: Fund earnings to cover gas, oil legal fees
summary: Fund earnings to cover gas, oil legal fees By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau Alaska State Legislature, Permanent Fund Division See related pages: http://www.legis.state.ak.us/ http://www.revenue.state.ak.us/pfd/ JUNEAU - State legislators have agreed to use about $1.5 million from earnings of the Permanent Fund to pay for oil and gas litigation, including legal review of BP Amoco's proposed purchase of the Atlantic Richfield Co. The House approved the spending for the Department of Law this week as part of the overall $2.1 billion general fund budget for fiscal year 2000. The possibility of using the politically sacred Permanent Fund to fund government operations has been a key issue in the Legislature this year, where several proposals to tap the fund for $1 billion or more are …

title: Avalanche hits Alyeska ski run
summary: Saturday, March 13, 1999 Avalanche hits Alyeska ski run Officials say no one badly hurt By KAREN AHO, LIZ RUSKIN and NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporters GIRDWOOD - An avalanche roared down a ski run at Alyeska Resort on Friday afternoon, partially burying at least two skiers and prompting a massive search for people who may have been trapped underneath. The effort, involving as many as 200 searchers and six rescue dogs high on a mountainside, was suspended between 8 and 9 p.m., and authorities said they were confident no one was still buried. "We have no reason to believe at this time that anyone is in the avalanche," Alyeska general manager Larry Daniels said Friday night. There were "absolutely no clues to indicate that there's anybody else," he said. No one was seriously hurt. The res…

title: Beluga hunting targeted
summary: Beluga hunting targeted Endangered status asked for whales By TOM BELL Daily News reporter Beluga whales A coalition of conservation groups and a former whale hunter filed a petition Wednesday asking the federal government to list Cook Inlet's belugas as an endangered species. The petitioners hope the designation will convince whale hunters to enact a voluntary moratorium on hunting when the white whales begin arriving in the upper Inlet next month. In addition, the group wants the state to withdraw plans to lease parts of Cook Inlet for oil and gas development. The state is planning to open up large sections of the Inlet for lease in April, said Kris Balliet, director of the Alaska office of the Center for Marine Conservation. The coalition delivered the 40-page petition late Wednesday af…

title: Park fishing reductions face state senators' opposition
summary: Park fishing reductions face state senators' opposition The Associated Press JUNEAU - State senators intend to protest a plan approved by Congress last year that would lead to a phase-out of commercial and subsistence fishing in Glacier Bay National Park. The Senate Resources Committee took three hours of testimony Wednesday, mostly from fishermen protesting the closure. Members of a work group sponsored by the state say the agreement was imperfect, the result of rushed, last-minute negotiating when Congress was trying to construct a budget bill. "We are being asked to load the gun, pull the hammer back and let them pull the trigger," said Dick Hofmann, president of the Alaska Trollers Association. The agreement, negotiated by U.S. Sen. Ted Stevens, R-Alaska, would allow many fishermen to …
